1:
"And it came to pass in the days of Amraphel king of Shinar, Arioch king of Ellasar, Chedorlaomer king of Elam, and Tidal king of nations;" The witnesses note the four eastern kings who made war. On "in the days of Amraphel king of Shinar," the grammatical voices note the kings. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "Amraphel king of Shinar, Arioch king of Ellasar, Chedorlaomer king of Elam, and Tidal king of nations" — four kings of the east (Mesopotamia and Elam) are named, the confederacy that warred against the cities of the plain; Shinar (Babylonia), Ellasar (a city of Babylonia), Elam (Persia/Elam, the leading power under Chedorlaomer), and "nations" (a king over various peoples); the historical setting of the war.

On the four kings, the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the confederacy. Clarke and Gill note that these eastern kings (with Chedorlaomer of Elam at their head, v.4-5) formed an alliance and came against the kings of the plain; the campaign of the great eastern powers into Canaan; the earliest recorded war in Scripture; the setting for Lot's capture (v.12) and Abram's rescue.

On "king of nations," the grammatical-historical note Tidal: "Tidal king of nations (Goiim)" — a king over various peoples or tribes; the term marks a ruler of mixed nations; the four-king confederacy of the east, led by Chedorlaomer, that subdued the cities of the plain and would carry off Lot. The verse displays the four eastern kings who made war.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the kings — "Amraphel king of Shinar, Arioch king of Ellasar, Chedorlaomer king of Elam, and Tidal king of nations," the four eastern kings (Keil, Ellicott); the confederacy led by Chedorlaomer (Clarke, Gill); "Tidal king of nations," a ruler of mixed peoples (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the eastern kings. The verse marks the kings: four. The verse displays the Shinar: and Elam. The verse displays the Chedorlaomer: the head. The verse displays the war: begun. The setting displays the eastern confederacy at war. The verse displays the kings: named. The verse displays the east: arrayed. The verse displays the war: the first recorded. The four eastern kings who made war display the setting of the campaign: Amraphel, Arioch, Chedorlaomer, and Tidal — the confederacy of the east, led by Chedorlaomer of Elam, that warred against the cities of the plain. The verse displays the eastern kings, against the five (v.2).
2:
"That these made war with Bera king of Sodom, and with Birsha king of Gomorrah, Shinab king of Admah, and Shemeber king of Zeboiim, and the king of Bela, which is Zoar." The brief verse notes the five kings of the plain against whom the war was made. The grammatical voices note the cities. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "Bera king of Sodom, and Birsha king of Gomorrah, Shinab king of Admah, and Shemeber king of Zeboiim, and the king of Bela (Zoar)" — the five kings of the cities of the plain (Sodom, Gomorrah, Admah, Zeboiim, and Bela/Zoar); the local kings who had been subject to Chedorlaomer (v.4) and now suffered his war; the cities of the plain, soon to be destroyed (ch. 19).

On the five kings, the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the alliance. Clarke and Gill note that the five kings of the plain were arrayed against the four eastern kings; the war of the four against the five; the cities of the plain, including Sodom (where Lot dwelt, 13:12), drawn into the conflict.

On "Bela, which is Zoar," the grammatical-historical note the name: "Bela (the same is Zoar)" — Bela, also called Zoar (the little city, to which Lot would later flee, 19:22); the fifth of the cities of the plain; the identification of the city. The verse displays the five kings of the plain.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the kings — "Bera king of Sodom... and the king of Bela, which is Zoar," the five kings of the plain (Keil, Ellicott); the cities subject to Chedorlaomer, now at war (Clarke, Gill); "Bela, which is Zoar," the fifth city, to which Lot would flee (19:22, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the kings of the plain. The verse marks the kings: five. The verse displays the Sodom: and Gomorrah. The verse displays the cities: of the plain. The verse displays the war: against the four. The array displays the five kings of the plain. The verse displays the kings: arrayed. The verse displays the cities: of the plain. The verse displays the Zoar: named. The five kings of the plain display the array against the eastern confederacy: Bera of Sodom and the kings of Gomorrah, Admah, Zeboiim, and Bela/Zoar — the five kings of the cities of the plain, against whom the four eastern kings made war. The verse displays the kings of the plain, joined in the vale of Siddim (v.3).
3:
"All these were joined together in the vale of Siddim, which is the salt sea." The very brief verse notes the joining of the kings in the vale of Siddim. The grammatical voices note the place. Ellicott and Gill note that "all these were joined together in the vale of Siddim, which is the salt sea" — the five kings of the plain joined together (allied, or gathered for battle) in the vale of Siddim; the valley which, the text notes, "is the salt sea" (the Dead Sea) — that is, the region later became (or was covered by) the salt sea; the place of the battle.

On "the vale of Siddim," the witnesses note the valley. Gill notes that the vale of Siddim was a fertile valley where the cities of the plain lay; the kings gathered there for battle against the eastern confederacy; the valley of the conflict.

On "which is the salt sea," the grammatical-historical note the later state: "which is the salt sea" — the narrator's note that the vale of Siddim is (or became) the salt sea (the Dead Sea); on one view, after the destruction of Sodom (ch. 19), the region was submerged and became part of the Dead Sea; the note marks the later geography; the place of the battle, later covered by the salt sea. The verse displays the joining of the kings in the vale of Siddim.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the joining — "all these were joined together in the vale of Siddim," the kings of the plain gathered for battle (Ellicott, Gill); the vale of Siddim, the valley of the cities (Gill); "which is the salt sea," the region later the Dead Sea (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the gathered kings. The verse marks the joining: in Siddim. The verse displays the vale: of the battle. The verse displays the salt sea: the later state. The verse displays the place: marked. The joining displays the kings gathered for battle in Siddim. The verse displays the vale: Siddim. The verse displays the kings: joined. The verse displays the sea: salt. The joining of the kings in the vale of Siddim displays the place of the battle: all these joined in the vale of Siddim, which is the salt sea — the kings of the plain gathered for battle in the valley later covered by the Dead Sea. The verse displays the gathered kings, after twelve years' service (v.4).
4:
"Twelve years they served Chedorlaomer, and in the thirteenth year they rebelled." The brief verse notes the cities' twelve years' servitude and their rebellion. The grammatical voices note the cause of the war. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "twelve years they served Chedorlaomer, and in the thirteenth year they rebelled" — the kings of the plain had been subject (tributary) to Chedorlaomer of Elam for twelve years; in the thirteenth year they rebelled (refused tribute, threw off his yoke); the rebellion that provoked Chedorlaomer's punitive campaign (v.5); the cause of the war.

On "twelve years they served Chedorlaomer," the witnesses note the subjection. Gill notes that the cities of the plain had been subject to the eastern king for twelve years; the dominion of Elam over the region; the servitude that the cities sought to cast off.

On "in the thirteenth year they rebelled," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the revolt: "in the thirteenth year they rebelled" — the cities revolted against Chedorlaomer; the rebellion brought the eastern kings back to subdue them; the occasion of the campaign (vv.5-7) and of Lot's capture (v.12); the political cause of the war that would draw in Abram. The verse displays the cities' servitude and rebellion.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the servitude — "twelve years they served Chedorlaomer, and in the thirteenth year they rebelled," the cities subject for twelve years, then rebelling (Keil, Gill); "twelve years they served Chedorlaomer," the dominion of Elam (Gill); "in the thirteenth year they rebelled," the revolt provoking the campaign (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the rebellion. The verse marks the service: twelve years. The verse displays the rebellion: thirteenth year. The verse displays the Chedorlaomer: served. The verse displays the war: provoked. The cause displays the cities' revolt bringing the war. The verse displays the servitude: twelve years. The verse displays the revolt: begun. The verse displays the campaign: provoked. The cities' twelve years' servitude and their rebellion display the cause of the war: twelve years they served Chedorlaomer, and in the thirteenth they rebelled — the revolt of the cities of the plain that provoked the eastern kings' campaign and led to Lot's capture. The verse displays the rebellion, met by Chedorlaomer's campaign (vv.5-7).
5:
"And in the fourteenth year came Chedorlaomer, and the kings that were with him, and smote the Rephaims in Ashteroth Karnaim, and the Zuzims in Ham, and the Emims in Shaveh Kiriathaim," The witnesses note Chedorlaomer's campaign against the peoples east of Jordan. On "in the fourteenth year came Chedorlaomer, and the kings that were with him," the grammatical voices note the campaign. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "in the fourteenth year came Chedorlaomer, and the kings that were with him" — in the fourteenth year (after the rebellion, v.4), Chedorlaomer and his allied kings marched to subdue the rebels and the surrounding peoples; the punitive campaign of the eastern confederacy.

On "smote the Rephaims in Ashteroth Karnaim, and the Zuzims in Ham, and the Emims in Shaveh Kiriathaim," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the peoples. Clarke and Ellicott note that the eastern kings smote various peoples east and south of the Dead Sea — the Rephaim (giants, at Ashteroth Karnaim), the Zuzim (at Ham), the Emim (at Shaveh Kiriathaim); ancient and powerful peoples (some of gigantic stature, Deuteronomy 2:10-11, 20); the campaign subdued the region.

On the peoples, the grammatical-historical note the sweep: the campaign of Chedorlaomer swept through the lands east of Jordan, smiting the Rephaim, Zuzim, and Emim; the eastern kings subduing the region before turning on the cities of the plain (v.8); the wide extent of the punitive war. The verse displays Chedorlaomer's campaign against the peoples east of Jordan.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the campaign — "in the fourteenth year came Chedorlaomer... and smote the Rephaims... the Zuzims... and the Emims," the eastern kings' punitive campaign (Keil, Gill); the peoples smitten — Rephaim, Zuzim, Emim (giants, Deuteronomy 2:10-11, Clarke, Ellicott); the wide sweep of the war (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the campaign. The verse marks the year: fourteenth. The verse displays the Chedorlaomer: marching. The verse displays the peoples: smitten. The verse displays the region: subdued. The campaign displays the eastern kings sweeping the region. The verse displays the Chedorlaomer: came. The verse displays the Rephaim: smitten. The verse displays the war: wide. Chedorlaomer's campaign against the peoples east of Jordan displays the punitive war: in the fourteenth year Chedorlaomer smote the Rephaim, Zuzim, and Emim — the eastern kings subduing the ancient peoples (giants, Deuteronomy 2:10-11) east of Jordan before turning on the cities of the plain. The verse displays the campaign, continued against the Horites (v.6).
6:
"And the Horites in their mount Seir, unto Elparan, which is by the wilderness." The very brief verse notes the smiting of the Horites in mount Seir. The grammatical voices note the campaign's reach. Gill and Clarke note that "the Horites in their mount Seir, unto Elparan, which is by the wilderness" — the eastern kings also smote the Horites (the cave-dwellers, the original inhabitants of mount Seir, later dispossessed by Esau's descendants, Deuteronomy 2:12, 22) in their mountain region of Seir; the campaign reached as far as El-paran, by the wilderness (toward the south, near the desert); the southward extent of the war.

On "the Horites in their mount Seir," the witnesses note the people. Gill notes the Horites of Seir — the inhabitants of the mountainous region south-east of the Dead Sea; smitten by Chedorlaomer's campaign; the peoples of Seir subdued.

On "unto Elparan, which is by the wilderness," the grammatical-historical note the limit: "unto El-paran, which is by the wilderness" — the campaign reached El-paran (perhaps near the gulf of Aqaba, or the edge of the wilderness of Paran), the southern limit of the war; the eastern kings sweeping down through the lands east and south of the Dead Sea; the wide reach of the punitive campaign. The verse displays the smiting of the Horites in mount Seir.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the Horites — "the Horites in their mount Seir, unto Elparan, which is by the wilderness," the Horites of Seir smitten (Deuteronomy 2:12, Gill, Clarke); the people of mount Seir subdued (Gill); "unto El-paran, which is by the wilderness," the southern limit of the campaign (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the smitten Horites. The verse marks the Horites: of Seir. The verse displays the mount: Seir. The verse displays the El-paran: the limit. The verse displays the campaign: southward. The smiting displays the campaign reaching mount Seir and the wilderness. The verse displays the Horites: smitten. The verse displays the Seir: subdued. The verse displays the reach: wide. The smiting of the Horites in mount Seir displays the southward reach of the campaign: the Horites in mount Seir, unto El-paran by the wilderness — the eastern kings subduing the Horites of Seir and reaching the edge of the wilderness, the wide sweep of the war. The verse displays the campaign, returning to Kadesh (v.7).
7:
"And they returned, and came to Enmishpat, which is Kadesh, and smote all the country of the Amalekites, and also the Amorites, that dwelt in Hazezontamar." The brief verse notes the eastern kings' return and their smiting of the Amalekites and Amorites. The grammatical voices note the turn. Ellicott and Gill note that "they returned, and came to Enmishpat, which is Kadesh, and smote all the country of the Amalekites, and also the Amorites, that dwelt in Hazezontamar" — the eastern kings turned back (from the southern limit) and came to En-mishpat (Kadesh), smiting the country of the Amalekites and the Amorites of Hazezon-tamar (En-gedi); the campaign sweeping back northward toward the cities of the plain.

On "the Amalekites," the witnesses note the anticipation. Gill notes that "the country of the Amalekites" is named by anticipation (the Amalekites, descendants of Esau's grandson Amalek, 36:12, were not yet in being in Abram's day; the country is named by the name it later bore); the region later held by the Amalekites; the campaign smote that country.

On "the Amorites, that dwelt in Hazezontamar," the grammatical-historical note the place: "the Amorites... in Hazezon-tamar" (En-gedi, by the Dead Sea) — the Amorites of that place smitten; the campaign drawing near the cities of the plain (Sodom, etc.); the eastern kings, having subdued the surrounding peoples, now turned on the rebellious cities (v.8). The verse displays the eastern kings' return and their smiting of the Amalekites and Amorites.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the return — "they returned, and came to Enmishpat, which is Kadesh, and smote all the country of the Amalekites, and also the Amorites," the kings turning back and smiting the peoples (Ellicott, Gill); "the Amalekites," the country named by anticipation (36:12, Gill); "the Amorites... in Hazezon-tamar," En-gedi by the Dead Sea (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the returning kings. The verse marks the return: to Kadesh. The verse displays the Amalekites: smitten. The verse displays the Amorites: of En-gedi. The verse displays the plain: drawn near. The return displays the kings turning toward the cities of the plain. The verse displays the kings: returning. The verse displays the peoples: smitten. The verse displays the war: nearing Sodom. The eastern kings' return and their smiting of the Amalekites and Amorites display the campaign nearing the plain: they returned to Kadesh and smote the Amalekites and the Amorites of Hazezon-tamar — the kings, having subdued the surrounding peoples, turning on the rebellious cities of the plain. The verse displays the kings, joined in battle by the plain-kings (vv.8-9).
8:
"And there went out the king of Sodom, and the king of Gomorrah, and the king of Admah, and the king of Zeboiim, and the king of Bela (the same is Zoar;) and they joined battle with them in the vale of Siddim;" The brief verse notes the five kings of the plain going out to battle. The grammatical voices note the array. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "there went out the king of Sodom, and the king of Gomorrah... and the king of Bela (Zoar); and they joined battle with them in the vale of Siddim" — the five kings of the plain went out and arrayed themselves for battle against the four eastern kings in the vale of Siddim (v.3); the local kings, facing the punitive campaign, gave battle; the engagement of the four kings against the five.

On "the king of Sodom... and the king of Bela," the witnesses note the kings. Gill notes the five kings of the plain arrayed for battle; the cities of the plain making their stand against Chedorlaomer's confederacy; the desperate defense against the eastern power.

On "they joined battle with them in the vale of Siddim," the grammatical-historical note the engagement: "they joined battle... in the vale of Siddim" — the battle was joined in the valley (later the salt sea, v.3); the five kings against the four; the engagement that the plain-kings would lose (v.10), leading to the sack of Sodom and Lot's capture (vv.11-12). The verse displays the five kings of the plain going out to battle.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the array — "there went out the king of Sodom... and they joined battle with them in the vale of Siddim," the five kings arrayed for battle (Keil, Gill); the cities making their stand (Gill); "they joined battle... in the vale of Siddim," the engagement of four against five (v.3, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the arrayed kings. The verse marks the kings: five. The verse displays the battle: joined. The verse displays the Siddim: the field. The verse displays the four: against five. The array displays the plain-kings giving battle. The verse displays the kings: out. The verse displays the battle: joined. The verse displays the defeat: impending. The five kings of the plain going out to battle display the engagement: the kings of Sodom, Gomorrah, Admah, Zeboiim, and Bela joined battle in the vale of Siddim — the five kings of the plain making their stand against the four eastern kings, the battle that would be lost. The verse displays the joined battle, four kings against five (v.9).
9:
"With Chedorlaomer the king of Elam, and with Tidal king of nations, and Amraphel king of Shinar, and Arioch king of Ellasar; four kings with five." The brief verse notes the four eastern kings arrayed against the five. The grammatical voices note the array. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "with Chedorlaomer the king of Elam, and with Tidal king of nations, and Amraphel king of Shinar, and Arioch king of Ellasar; four kings with five" — the four eastern kings (named again, with Chedorlaomer of Elam at their head) were arrayed against the five kings of the plain; "four kings with (against) five" — the disproportion noted, yet the four prevailed; the engagement of the two confederacies.

On "Chedorlaomer the king of Elam," the witnesses note the leader. Gill notes that Chedorlaomer of Elam led the eastern confederacy; the chief of the four kings, to whom the cities had been subject (v.4); the leader of the campaign.

On "four kings with five," the grammatical-historical note the disproportion: "four kings with five" — the four eastern kings, fewer in number, faced the five kings of the plain; yet the four (the seasoned eastern power) defeated the five (v.10); the note marks the odds; the victory of the four, who carried off the spoil and Lot (vv.11-12); the setting for Abram's rescue with a mere three hundred eighteen men (v.14), a greater disproportion still. The verse displays the four eastern kings arrayed against the five.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the array — "with Chedorlaomer... four kings with five," the four eastern kings against the five of the plain (Keil, Gill); "Chedorlaomer the king of Elam," the leader of the confederacy (v.4, Gill); "four kings with five," the disproportion, yet the four prevailed (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the arrayed kings. The verse marks the kings: four. The verse displays the Chedorlaomer: the head. The verse displays the five: opposed. The verse displays the odds: four with five. The array displays the eastern confederacy against the plain-kings. The verse displays the four: arrayed. The verse displays the five: faced. The verse displays the battle: set. The four eastern kings arrayed against the five display the engagement: Chedorlaomer, Tidal, Amraphel, and Arioch, four kings with five — the eastern confederacy, fewer in number, facing the five kings of the plain, yet prevailing. The verse displays the four kings, victorious in the slimepits (v.10).
10:
"And the vale of Siddim was full of slimepits; and the kings of Sodom and Gomorrah fled, and fell there; and they that remained fled to the mountain." The witnesses note the defeat and flight of the kings of the plain. On "the vale of Siddim was full of slimepits," the grammatical voices note the bitumen pits. Ellicott and Gill note that "the vale of Siddim was full of slimepits (bitumen pits)" — the valley was full of pits of bitumen (asphalt, plentiful in that region); the pits became a snare in the battle and flight; the natural hazard of the valley that turned the rout into disaster.

On "the kings of Sodom and Gomorrah fled, and fell there," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the rout. Calvin and Gill note that "the kings of Sodom and Gomorrah fled, and fell there" — the kings of the plain, defeated, fled and fell into the slimepits (or fell/perished there); the rout of the plain-kings; their flight into the very pits of the valley; the disaster of the defeat (the king of Sodom, it seems, escaped, since he appears later, v.17).

On "they that remained fled to the mountain," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the escape: "they that remained fled to the mountain" — the survivors fled to the hills for refuge; the scattering of the defeated; the rout complete, the cities open to plunder (v.11), and Lot taken (v.12). The verse displays the defeat and flight of the kings of the plain.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the slimepits — "the vale of Siddim was full of slimepits," the bitumen pits, a snare in the flight (Ellicott, Gill); "the kings of Sodom and Gomorrah fled, and fell there," the rout of the plain-kings (Calvin, Gill); "they that remained fled to the mountain," the survivors fleeing to the hills (Clarke, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the defeat. The verse marks the slimepits: full. The verse displays the kings: fled and fell. The verse displays the remnant: to the mountain. The verse displays the rout: complete. The defeat displays the plain-kings routed in the valley. The verse displays the kings: fallen. The verse displays the pits: a snare. The verse displays the rout: total. The defeat and flight of the kings of the plain display the disaster of the battle: the vale was full of slimepits, the kings fled and fell, and the rest fled to the mountain — the rout of the plain-kings, the cities left open to plunder and Lot's capture. The verse displays the defeated plain, plundered (v.11).
11:
"And they took all the goods of Sodom and Gomorrah, and all their victuals, and went their way." The very brief verse notes the plundering of Sodom and Gomorrah. The grammatical voices note the spoil. Gill and Clarke note that "they took all the goods of Sodom and Gomorrah, and all their victuals, and went their way" — the victorious eastern kings plundered the defeated cities, taking all their goods and provisions (victuals), and departed; the sack of Sodom and Gomorrah; the spoil carried off by the conquerors; the cities stripped.

On "all the goods of Sodom and Gomorrah, and all their victuals," the witnesses note the plunder. Gill notes that the eastern kings took the goods and food of the cities; the complete plundering of the defeated; the wealth and provisions of the plain carried away.

On "went their way," the grammatical-historical note the departure: "went their way" — the conquerors, laden with spoil (and with captives, including Lot, v.12), departed northward toward their own lands; the retreat of the victorious kings; the setting for Abram's pursuit (v.14); the plunderers carrying off the goods of Sodom, and Lot among the captives. The verse displays the plundering of Sodom and Gomorrah.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the spoil — "they took all the goods of Sodom and Gomorrah, and all their victuals, and went their way," the plundering of the cities (Gill, Clarke); "all the goods... and all their victuals," the complete plunder (Gill); "went their way," the conquerors departing with the spoil (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the plundered cities. The verse marks the goods: taken. The verse displays the victuals: seized. The verse displays the cities: stripped. The verse displays the conquerors: departing. The plunder displays the sack of Sodom and Gomorrah. The verse displays the goods: carried off. The verse displays the cities: plundered. The verse displays the way: taken. The plundering of Sodom and Gomorrah displays the sack of the cities: they took all the goods and victuals of Sodom and Gomorrah and went their way — the victorious eastern kings plundering the defeated cities and departing with the spoil, and with Lot among the captives. The verse displays the plunderers, carrying off Lot (v.12).
12:
"And they took Lot, Abram's brother's son, who dwelt in Sodom, and his goods, and departed." The witnesses note the capture of Lot, who dwelt in Sodom. On "they took Lot, Abram's brother's son," the grammatical voices note the captive. Gill and Calvin note that "they took Lot, Abram's brother's son... and his goods, and departed" — among the captives and spoil, the eastern kings took Lot (Abram's nephew) and his goods; Lot, caught up in the disaster of Sodom; the capture that would draw Abram into the war.

On "who dwelt in Sodom," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note Lot's declension. Calvin and Gill note the telling phrase — "who dwelt in Sodom"; Lot, who had at first pitched his tent toward Sodom (13:12), now dwelt in the city itself; his gradual drawing into Sodom; and now he shared in Sodom's calamity, taken captive with the city; the consequence of his worldly choice (13:10-11) — he who chose the plain for its richness now suffered with the wicked city; the danger of dwelling among the ungodly.

On "and his goods, and departed," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the loss: "and his goods, and departed" — Lot lost his goods (the very wealth for which he had chosen Sodom's plain) and his liberty; the worldly gain turned to loss; the captive carried off; the providence that would bring Abram to his rescue (v.14), and would, in time, draw Lot out of Sodom before its destruction (ch. 19). The verse displays the capture of Lot, who dwelt in Sodom.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the captive — "they took Lot, Abram's brother's son... and his goods, and departed," Lot taken captive with his goods (Gill, Calvin); "who dwelt in Sodom," Lot's declension from pitching toward Sodom (13:12) to dwelling in it, sharing its calamity (Calvin, Gill); "and his goods," the loss of his worldly gain (Clarke, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the captive Lot. The verse marks the Lot: taken. The verse displays the Sodom: where he dwelt. The verse displays the goods: lost. The verse displays the declension: into Sodom. The capture displays Lot suffering with the city he chose. The verse displays the Lot: captive. The verse displays the worldly choice: its fruit. The verse displays the loss: of goods. The capture of Lot, who dwelt in Sodom, displays the fruit of his worldly choice: they took Lot, Abram's nephew, who dwelt in Sodom, and his goods — the man who chose the plain for its richness now taken captive with the wicked city, his gain turned to loss. The verse displays the captive Lot, whose taking draws in Abram (v.13).
13:
"And there came one that had escaped, and told Abram the Hebrew; for he dwelt in the plain of Mamre the Amorite, brother of Eshcol, and brother of Aner: and these were confederate with Abram." The witnesses note the report brought to Abram the Hebrew and his confederates. On "there came one that had escaped, and told Abram the Hebrew," the grammatical voices note the report. Ellicott and Gill note that "there came one that had escaped, and told Abram the Hebrew" — a fugitive from the battle brought word to Abram of Lot's capture; Abram is here first called "the Hebrew" (ha-ibri, perhaps from Eber, 10:21, 24, or "the one from beyond [the river]"); the report that moved Abram to act.

On "Abram the Hebrew," the puritan Henry and witnesses note the title. Henry and Gill note that Abram is called "the Hebrew" — the descendant of Eber, or the man "from beyond the Euphrates"; the title marks him as a distinct people, a stranger and sojourner among the Canaanites; the name by which the chosen line would be known.

On "for he dwelt in the plain of Mamre the Amorite... and these were confederate with Abram," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the allies: Abram dwelt at Mamre (13:18), and Mamre, Eshcol, and Aner (Amorite chiefs) were "confederate with Abram" — allied with him; the patriarch had made a league with these local Amorites; they would join him in the rescue (v.24); the alliances of Abram in the land, and the help they gave. The verse displays the report brought to Abram the Hebrew and his confederates.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the report — "there came one that had escaped, and told Abram the Hebrew," a fugitive bringing word of Lot's capture (Ellicott, Gill); "Abram the Hebrew," the title from Eber or "from beyond" (10:21, Henry, Gill); "these were confederate with Abram," Mamre, Eshcol, and Aner allied with Abram (v.24, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the informed Abram. The verse marks the report: of Lot's capture. The verse displays the Hebrew: Abram. The verse displays the confederates: Amorite chiefs. The verse displays the alliance: with Abram. The report displays Abram learning of Lot's capture and his allies. The verse displays the Abram: the Hebrew. The verse displays the allies: confederate. The verse displays the rescue: prompted. The report brought to Abram the Hebrew and his confederates displays the prompting of the rescue: a fugitive told Abram the Hebrew, who dwelt at Mamre with his Amorite confederates — the report of Lot's capture moving the patriarch, with his allies, to act. The verse displays the informed Abram, arming his servants (v.14).
14:
"And when Abram heard that his brother was taken captive, he armed his trained servants, born in his own house, three hundred and eighteen, and pursued them unto Dan." The witnesses note Abram arming his servants to rescue Lot. On "when Abram heard that his brother was taken captive," the grammatical voices note the resolve. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "when Abram heard that his brother (kinsman, nephew) was taken captive, he armed his trained servants" — Abram, hearing of Lot's capture, resolved to rescue him; the patriarch's love and loyalty to his kinsman (despite Lot's earlier choice, ch. 13); he acted promptly to deliver Lot.

On "he armed his trained servants, born in his own house, three hundred and eighteen," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the band. Clarke and Ellicott note that Abram "armed his trained servants (his trained men, his retainers), born in his own house, three hundred and eighteen" — Abram's own household servants, born and bred in his house, trained and trusty, numbering three hundred eighteen; the considerable household of the patriarch (a sign of his greatness); the small band with which he would pursue the victorious kings; the disproportion (318 men against the armies of four kings) marking the faith and courage of Abram, and the help of God.

On "pursued them unto Dan," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the pursuit: "pursued them unto Dan" — Abram pursued the retreating kings as far as Dan (in the north of Canaan; the place named by anticipation, by the name it later bore, Judges 18:29); the swift pursuit of the patriarch; the faith and resolve that led him to attack a far greater force. The verse displays Abram arming his servants to rescue Lot.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the resolve — "when Abram heard that his brother was taken captive, he armed his trained servants," Abram acting to rescue Lot (Keil, Gill); "three hundred and eighteen," the trained household servants, a small band (Clarke, Ellicott); "pursued them unto Dan," the swift pursuit (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the armed Abram. The verse marks the captive: Lot. The verse displays the servants: 318 trained. The verse displays the pursuit: unto Dan. The verse displays the faith: and courage. The resolve displays Abram pursuing with his small band. The verse displays the Abram: armed. The verse displays the servants: trained. The verse displays the pursuit: swift. Abram arming his servants to rescue Lot displays the faith and loyalty of the patriarch: hearing of Lot's capture, Abram armed his 318 trained servants and pursued the kings unto Dan — the patriarch, with a small band, in faith and courage, going to deliver his kinsman from a far greater force. The verse displays the pursuing Abram, smiting the kings by night (v.15).
15:
"And he divided himself against them, he and his servants, by night, and smote them, and pursued them unto Hobah, which is on the left hand of Damascus." The witnesses note Abram's night attack and his smiting of the kings. On "he divided himself against them, he and his servants, by night," the grammatical voices note the stratagem. Ellicott and Gill note that "he divided himself against them, he and his servants, by night, and smote them" — Abram divided his small force (a military stratagem, attacking from several sides) and fell upon the enemy by night; the surprise night attack; the patriarch's wisdom and courage in the assault; the small band, by stratagem and God's help, routed the victorious kings.

On "smote them, and pursued them unto Hobah," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the rout. Clarke and Gill note that Abram "smote them, and pursued them unto Hobah, which is on the left hand (north) of Damascus" — the night attack routed the enemy, and Abram pursued them far to the north, beyond Damascus; the victory of the patriarch over a far greater force; the rout of the four kings; the deliverance accomplished.

On the victory, the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the help of God: Abram's victory over the four kings (with their armies) by a band of three hundred eighteen was the work of God; the faith of the patriarch, and the divine help that gave him the victory; the LORD delivered the enemies into his hand (v.20); the deliverance of Lot accomplished by God's power through Abram's faith and courage. The verse displays Abram's night attack and his smiting of the kings.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the stratagem — "he divided himself against them, he and his servants, by night, and smote them," the surprise night attack (Ellicott, Gill); "pursued them unto Hobah, which is on the left hand of Damascus," the rout, pursued beyond Damascus (Clarke, Gill); the victory the work of God (v.20, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the victorious Abram. The verse marks the attack: by night. The verse displays the stratagem: divided. The verse displays the rout: smitten. The verse displays the help: of God. The attack displays Abram routing the kings by night. The verse displays the Abram: smiting. The verse displays the kings: routed. The verse displays the victory: God-given. Abram's night attack and his smiting of the kings display the God-given victory: Abram divided his band, fell on them by night, smote them, and pursued them beyond Damascus — the patriarch, by stratagem and God's help, routing the four kings and delivering Lot. The verse displays the victorious Abram, bringing back Lot and the spoil (v.16).
16:
"And he brought back all the goods, and also brought again his brother Lot, and his goods, and the women also, and the people." The brief verse notes Abram's recovery of Lot, the captives, and the goods. The grammatical voices note the deliverance. Gill and Clarke note that "he brought back all the goods, and also brought again his brother Lot, and his goods, and the women also, and the people" — Abram recovered all that the kings had taken: the goods of Sodom, his nephew Lot and his goods, and the women and people (the captives); the complete deliverance; the patriarch restored all the spoil and freed all the captives.

On "brought again his brother Lot, and his goods," the witnesses note Lot's rescue. Gill notes that Abram especially "brought again his brother Lot, and his goods" — the rescue of Lot, the object of the expedition; the nephew delivered from captivity; Abram's love and loyalty rewarded with success.

On "the women also, and the people," the grammatical-historical note the full recovery: "the women also, and the people" — all the captives, women and men, were freed; the complete deliverance of the people of Sodom and the spoil; the victory full and the recovery total; the patriarch, having routed the kings, restored all that was lost. The verse displays Abram's recovery of Lot, the captives, and the goods.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the recovery — "he brought back all the goods, and also brought again his brother Lot, and his goods, and the women also, and the people," the complete deliverance (Gill, Clarke); "brought again his brother Lot," the rescue of Lot, the object of the expedition (Gill); "the women also, and the people," the full recovery of the captives (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the recovered spoil. The verse marks the goods: brought back. The verse displays the Lot: rescued. The verse displays the women: freed. The verse displays the people: restored. The recovery displays Abram restoring all that was taken. The verse displays the Abram: recovering. The verse displays the Lot: delivered. The verse displays the captives: freed. Abram's recovery of Lot, the captives, and the goods displays the full deliverance: Abram brought back all the goods, Lot and his goods, the women, and the people — the complete recovery of the spoil and the captives, Lot delivered, the patriarch's rescue accomplished. The verse displays the victorious Abram, met by the king of Sodom (v.17).
17:
"And the king of Sodom went out to meet him after his return from the slaughter of Chedorlaomer, and of the kings that were with him, at the valley of Shaveh, which is the king's dale." The witnesses note the king of Sodom going out to meet the returning Abram. On "the king of Sodom went out to meet him after his return from the slaughter of Chedorlaomer," the grammatical voices note the meeting. Ellicott and Gill note that "the king of Sodom went out to meet him after his return from the slaughter of Chedorlaomer, and of the kings" — the king of Sodom (who had survived, v.10) came out to meet Abram, victorious and returning with the spoil; the meeting that would lead to the king's offer (v.21) and Abram's refusal (vv.22-23); the welcome of the victor.

On "after his return from the slaughter of Chedorlaomer, and of the kings," the witnesses note the victory. Gill notes that Abram returned "from the slaughter of Chedorlaomer, and of the kings that were with him" — from the defeat of the four kings; the victorious patriarch, having routed the eastern confederacy; the conqueror returning.

On "at the valley of Shaveh, which is the king's dale," the grammatical-historical note the place: "the valley of Shaveh, which is the king's dale" — a valley near Salem (Jerusalem), later called the king's dale (cf. 2 Samuel 18:18); the place of the meeting, where both the king of Sodom and Melchizedek (v.18) came to Abram; the setting for the contrast between the two kings — the king of Sodom and the king of Salem. The verse displays the king of Sodom going out to meet Abram.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the meeting — "the king of Sodom went out to meet him after his return from the slaughter of Chedorlaomer," the king of Sodom meeting the victorious Abram (Ellicott, Gill); "from the slaughter of Chedorlaomer, and of the kings," the victory over the four kings (Gill); "the valley of Shaveh, which is the king's dale," the place near Salem (2 Samuel 18:18, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the meeting. The verse marks the king: of Sodom. The verse displays the meeting: the victor. The verse displays the slaughter: of the kings. The verse displays the dale: the king's. The meeting displays the king of Sodom welcoming Abram. The verse displays the Sodom-king: out. The verse displays the Abram: returning. The verse displays the place: the king's dale. The king of Sodom going out to meet Abram displays the welcome of the victor: the king of Sodom met Abram, returning from the slaughter of the kings, in the king's dale — the setting for the meeting with Melchizedek and the contrast of the two kings. The verse displays the met Abram, blessed by Melchizedek (v.18).
18:
"And Melchizedek king of Salem brought forth bread and wine: and he was the priest of the most high God." The witnesses note Melchizedek, king of Salem and priest of the most high God — the great typological crux. On "Melchizedek king of Salem," interpreters divide over his identity. The grammatical-historical and many hold he was a real, historical king-priest of Salem (Jerusalem), a worshipper of the true God (El Elyon) amid the Canaanites, who, as both king and priest, was a type of Christ (Hebrews 7); "Melchizedek" means "king of righteousness," and "king of Salem" means "king of peace" (Hebrews 7:2); without recorded genealogy, beginning, or end of life in the narrative, he prefigures Christ's eternal, royal priesthood. A Jewish tradition (noted by Gill) identifies Melchizedek with Shem the son of Noah (still living); but this is rejected by many, since Shem's parentage and pedigree are known (whereas Melchizedek is "without father, without mother, without descent," Hebrews 7:3). Some have held he was Christ himself (a Christophany), but the dominant Christian view (with Hebrews 7) takes him as a real man, a type of Christ.

On "brought forth bread and wine," interpreters note the act. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke and the dominant reading hold the bread and wine were brought to refresh Abram and his weary men (a hospitable provision after the battle); Clarke rejects as "an idle conceit" the notion that Melchizedek offered them in the way of sacrifice. A second reading (the Roman Catholic) takes the bread and wine as a priestly offering or sacrifice, prefiguring the Eucharist; but the natural sense, and Hebrews (which dwells on Melchizedek's blessing and tithe, not on bread and wine as a sacrifice), supports the refreshment-reading.

On "he was the priest of the most high God," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the priesthood: Melchizedek "was the priest of the most high God (El Elyon)" — a true priest of the true God, outside the line of Abraham and before the Levitical priesthood; his priesthood, royal and not by descent, is the pattern of Christ's priesthood ("after the order of Melchizedek," Psalm 110:4; Hebrews 5-7); the king-priest who blessed Abram and received his tithe, a type of the royal Priest, Christ. The verse displays Melchizedek, king of Salem and priest of the most high God.

The witnesses agree the substance: Melchizedek, king of Salem and priest of the most high God, brought bread and wine, and was a type of Christ (Hebrews 7) (Gill, Keil, Ellicott, Calvin, Clarke). His identity bears a crux: a real Canaanite king-priest of Salem, type of Christ (the dominant view, Hebrews 7) vs Shem the son of Noah (the Jewish tradition, rejected by many) vs Christ himself; and the bread and wine: a refreshment for Abram's men (the dominant reading, Clarke) vs a priestly offering/sacrifice prefiguring the Eucharist. The verse displays the Melchizedek crux. The verse marks the king: of Salem. The verse displays the debate: real king-priest, Shem, or Christ; refreshment or sacrifice. The verse displays the priest: of the most high God. The verse displays the type: of Christ. Melchizedek displays the king-priest and a crux: Melchizedek, king of Salem, priest of the most high God, brought bread and wine — read as a real king-priest and type of Christ (Hebrews 7; the dominant view), as Shem (the Jewish tradition), or as Christ himself; the bread and wine a refreshment (Clarke) or a sacrificial offering. The verse displays Melchizedek, blessing Abram (v.19).
19:
"And he blessed him, and said, Blessed be Abram of the most high God, possessor of heaven and earth:" The witnesses note Melchizedek's blessing of Abram. On "he blessed him," the grammatical voices note the blessing. Ellicott and Gill note that "he blessed him" — Melchizedek, as priest of the most high God, blessed Abram; the priestly blessing; "the less is blessed of the better" (Hebrews 7:7), so Melchizedek (the type of Christ) was greater than Abram, blessing him; the king-priest pronouncing God's blessing on the patriarch.

On "Blessed be Abram of the most high God," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the benediction. Calvin and Gill note that Melchizedek pronounced, "Blessed be Abram of the most high God" — invoking God's blessing on Abram; the patriarch blessed in the name of the true God; the priestly benediction acknowledging Abram as blessed of God.

On "possessor of heaven and earth," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the title of God: "the most high God, possessor (maker, owner) of heaven and earth" — Melchizedek named God as El Elyon, the Most High, the possessor (creator and owner) of heaven and earth; the confession of the one true God, sovereign over all; the title that Abram would echo (v.22); the worship of the Most High amid the Canaanites; the true theology of Melchizedek the priest. The verse displays Melchizedek's blessing of Abram.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the blessing — "he blessed him," Melchizedek blessing Abram, the less blessed of the better (Hebrews 7:7, Ellicott, Gill); "Blessed be Abram of the most high God," the priestly benediction (Calvin, Gill); "possessor of heaven and earth," God named the Most High, maker of all (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the blessing Melchizedek. The verse marks the blessing: of Abram. The verse displays the Most High: God. The verse displays the possessor: of heaven and earth. The verse displays the priest: greater. The blessing displays Melchizedek blessing Abram in God's name. The verse displays the Melchizedek: blessing. The verse displays the Abram: blessed. The verse displays the God: Most High. Melchizedek's blessing of Abram displays the priestly benediction: he blessed Abram of the most high God, possessor of heaven and earth — the king-priest, type of Christ, blessing the patriarch in the name of the true God, the Most High, maker of all. The verse displays the blessing Melchizedek, blessing God and receiving tithes (v.20).
20:
"And blessed be the most high God, which hath delivered thine enemies into thy hand. And he gave him tithes of all." The witnesses note Melchizedek blessing God and Abram giving him tithes. On "blessed be the most high God, which hath delivered thine enemies into thy hand," the grammatical voices note the praise. Ellicott and Gill note that "blessed be the most high God, which hath delivered thine enemies into thy hand" — Melchizedek blessed (praised) God for giving Abram the victory; he ascribed the deliverance not to Abram's prowess but to God; the priest's thanksgiving, acknowledging God as the author of the victory; the true confession that God delivered the enemies into Abram's hand.

On "blessed be the most high God," the reformed Calvin notes the thanksgiving. Calvin and Gill note that Melchizedek gave the glory of the victory to God; the praise of the Most High for the deliverance; the priest leading in thanksgiving; the acknowledgment that the victory was God's gift, not man's achievement.

On "he gave him tithes of all," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the tithe: "he gave him tithes of all" — Abram gave Melchizedek a tenth of all (the spoil); the patriarch, acknowledging Melchizedek's priesthood and superior dignity, paid him tithes (Hebrews 7:4-10); the tithe a recognition of Melchizedek as priest of God (and so a type of Christ, to whom Abram, and in him Levi, paid tithes); the act marking Melchizedek's greatness and the honor due to God's priest. The verse displays Melchizedek blessing God and Abram's tithe.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the praise — "blessed be the most high God, which hath delivered thine enemies into thy hand," Melchizedek praising God for the victory (Ellicott, Gill); the glory given to God, not Abram (Calvin, Gill); "he gave him tithes of all," Abram's tithe to Melchizedek, acknowledging his priesthood (Hebrews 7:4-10, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the praising Melchizedek. The verse marks the praise: blessed be God. The verse displays the victory: God's gift. The verse displays the tithe: of all. The verse displays the priesthood: acknowledged. The act displays Melchizedek praising God and Abram tithing. The verse displays the God: praised. The verse displays the Abram: tithing. The verse displays the priest: honored. Melchizedek blessing God and Abram giving him tithes display the worship of the king-priest: Melchizedek blessed God for the victory, and Abram gave him tithes of all — the priest ascribing the deliverance to God, and the patriarch honoring Melchizedek's priesthood (Hebrews 7:4-10) with a tenth of the spoil. The verse displays the honored Melchizedek, before the king of Sodom's offer (v.21).
21:
"And the king of Sodom said unto Abram, Give me the persons, and take the goods to thyself." The witnesses note the king of Sodom's offer to Abram. On "the king of Sodom said unto Abram, Give me the persons," the grammatical voices note the request. Ellicott and Gill note that "the king of Sodom said unto Abram, Give me the persons (the souls, the people), and take the goods to thyself" — the king of Sodom asked Abram to return the persons (his people, the captives Abram had recovered) but to keep the goods (the spoil) for himself; the offer that, by the law of war, the recovered spoil belonged to the victor; the king's proposal.

On "Give me the persons," the puritan Henry and witnesses note the request for the people. Henry and Gill note that the king asked for his people (the captives) back; the persons being of more value to the king than the goods; the request that Abram restore the people and keep the spoil.

On "take the goods to thyself," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the offer of spoil: "take the goods to thyself" — the king offered Abram the goods (the spoil he had recovered) as his by right of conquest; the temptation to enrich himself with the goods of Sodom; the offer that Abram would refuse (vv.22-23), lest the wicked king should claim to have made him rich; the test of Abram's disinterestedness. The verse displays the king of Sodom's offer to Abram.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the request — "the king of Sodom said unto Abram, Give me the persons, and take the goods to thyself," the king asking for his people and offering Abram the spoil (Ellicott, Gill); "Give me the persons," the request for the captives (Henry, Gill); "take the goods to thyself," the offer of spoil, which Abram would refuse (vv.22-23, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the king's offer. The verse marks the persons: requested. The verse displays the goods: offered. The verse displays the king: of Sodom. The verse displays the test: of Abram. The offer displays the king proposing to give Abram the spoil. The verse displays the persons: asked. The verse displays the goods: offered. The verse displays the refusal: coming. The king of Sodom's offer to Abram displays the proposal and the test: give me the persons and take the goods to thyself — the king asking for his people and offering Abram the spoil by right of conquest, the temptation that Abram would refuse. The verse displays the king's offer, refused by Abram (vv.22-23).
22:
"And Abram said to the king of Sodom, I have lift up mine hand unto the LORD, the most high God, the possessor of heaven and earth," The witnesses note Abram's oath to the LORD in refusing the king's offer. On "I have lift up mine hand unto the LORD," the grammatical voices note the oath. Gill and Clarke note that "I have lift up mine hand unto the LORD" — Abram declared that he had sworn (lifting up the hand being the gesture of an oath) to the LORD; the patriarch had bound himself by oath; the solemn resolve, made before God, governing his refusal of the spoil (v.23).

On "the LORD, the most high God, the possessor of heaven and earth," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the confession. Calvin and Gill note that Abram swore by "the LORD (Jehovah), the most high God, the possessor of heaven and earth" — echoing Melchizedek's title for God (v.19), Abram confessed the LORD as the Most High, maker and owner of all; the patriarch identified the true God (Jehovah) with the Most High whom Melchizedek served; the confession of the one true God before the king of Sodom; Abram's God the possessor of all, who needed not the gifts of the king.

On the oath, the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the resolve: Abram's oath to the LORD, the possessor of heaven and earth, was the ground of his refusal; trusting God who possessed all things, the patriarch would take nothing from the king of Sodom (v.23); the faith and integrity of Abram, who looked to God, not man, for his enrichment. The verse displays Abram's oath to the LORD.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the oath — "I have lift up mine hand unto the LORD," Abram's solemn oath before God (Gill, Clarke); "the LORD, the most high God, the possessor of heaven and earth," Abram echoing Melchizedek's confession of the true God (v.19, Calvin, Gill); the oath the ground of his refusal (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the swearing Abram. The verse marks the oath: lifted hand. The verse displays the LORD: the Most High. The verse displays the possessor: of all. The verse displays the refusal: grounded. The oath displays Abram bound to God in refusing the spoil. The verse displays the Abram: sworn. The verse displays the God: confessed. The verse displays the integrity: shown. Abram's oath to the LORD displays the ground of his refusal: I have lifted my hand to the LORD, the Most High, possessor of heaven and earth — Abram, echoing Melchizedek's confession (v.19), bound by oath to the true God who possesses all, refusing to take from the king of Sodom. The verse displays the sworn Abram, refusing a thread (v.23).
23:
"That I will not take from a thread even to a shoelatchet, and that I will not take any thing that is thine, lest thou shouldest say, I have made Abram rich:" The witnesses note Abram's refusal of the smallest part of Sodom's spoil. On "I will not take from a thread even to a shoelatchet," the grammatical voices note the total refusal. Gill and Clarke note that "I will not take from a thread even to a shoelatchet, and that I will not take any thing that is thine" — Abram refused to take anything of the king of Sodom's, not the smallest thing (from a thread to a shoe-lace); the complete, scrupulous refusal; the patriarch would not enrich himself with the goods of Sodom in the least particular.

On "I will not take any thing that is thine," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the disinterestedness. Calvin and Gill note that Abram would take nothing that belonged to the king; his refusal was total; the patriarch's noble disinterestedness, refusing the spoil he might have claimed by right; the faith that looked to God, not to the wealth of Sodom.

On "lest thou shouldest say, I have made Abram rich," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the reason: "lest thou shouldest say, I have made Abram rich" — Abram refused, lest the wicked king should boast that he had enriched the patriarch; Abram would owe his wealth to God alone (whose promise, 12:2, and gift were his riches), not to the king of Sodom; the concern for God's honor and his own integrity; that none might say man, not God, had made him rich; the magnanimity and faith of Abram. The verse displays Abram's refusal of the smallest part of Sodom's spoil.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the refusal — "I will not take from a thread even to a shoelatchet, and that I will not take any thing that is thine," Abram's total, scrupulous refusal (Gill, Clarke); "I will not take any thing that is thine," the patriarch's disinterestedness (Calvin, Gill); "lest thou shouldest say, I have made Abram rich," Abram refusing lest the king boast, owing his wealth to God alone (12:2, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the refusing Abram. The verse marks the refusal: from a thread. The verse displays the disinterestedness: total. The verse displays the reason: lest the king boast. The verse displays the riches: from God. The refusal displays Abram refusing the spoil for God's honor. The verse displays the Abram: refusing. The verse displays the spoil: declined. The verse displays the integrity: kept. Abram's refusal of the smallest part of Sodom's spoil displays his disinterested faith: I will not take from a thread to a shoelatchet, lest thou say, I have made Abram rich — the patriarch refusing all the spoil he might have claimed, that he might owe his wealth to God alone, not to the king of Sodom. The verse displays the refusing Abram, reserving only his allies' portion (v.24).
24:
"Save only that which the young men have eaten, and the portion of the men which went with me, Aner, Eshcol, and Mamre; let them take their portion." The witnesses note Abram's exception for his men and his allies' portion. On "Save only that which the young men have eaten," the grammatical voices note the exception. Ellicott and Gill note that "save only that which the young men have eaten, and the portion of the men which went with me" — Abram excepted only what his young men (servants) had already consumed (their necessary provisions during the expedition), and the portion due to his allies; the patriarch's refusal (v.23) extended to his own gain, but he would not impose his abstinence on others.

On "the portion of the men which went with me, Aner, Eshcol, and Mamre," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the allies. Clarke and Gill note that Abram reserved "the portion of the men which went with me, Aner, Eshcol, and Mamre" — his Amorite confederates (13:18; 14:13), who had joined the expedition, should take their rightful share of the spoil; Abram, while refusing for himself, would not deprive his allies of their due; the justice and fairness of the patriarch.

On "let them take their portion," the reformed and grammatical-historical note the equity: "let them take their portion" — Abram's abstinence was his own act of faith and disinterestedness, not a law imposed on his allies; he left them free to take their share; the patriarch's equity — generous to others while self-denying for himself; the balance of his integrity and his fairness. The verse displays Abram's exception for his men and allies.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the exception — "save only that which the young men have eaten, and the portion of the men which went with me," Abram's exception for provisions consumed and his allies' share (Ellicott, Gill); "Aner, Eshcol, and Mamre," the Amorite confederates' due portion (13:18; 14:13, Clarke, Gill); "let them take their portion," Abram's equity, not imposing his abstinence on others (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the equitable Abram. The verse marks the exception: provisions and allies. The verse displays the young men: their food. The verse displays the allies: their portion. The verse displays the equity: of Abram. The exception displays Abram fair to others while self-denying. The verse displays the Abram: equitable. The verse displays the allies: their share. The verse displays the integrity: balanced. Abram's exception for his men and his allies' portion displays his equity: save only what the young men ate and the portion of Aner, Eshcol, and Mamre — Abram, refusing for himself, yet just to his men and allies, letting them take their due share. The verse closes the chapter, before the word of the LORD to Abram (ch.15).
